schools.summary: Matthew Henry turns the inventory into a lesson in fidelity: the temple sanctifies the gold, dedicated things must not be alienated, and the unchanged ark shows God's presence the same in small assemblies as great — the ark a type of Christ.
Clarke surveys the chapter's movement from the consecrated treasures to the glory-cloud; Wesley notes the gold was the remainder of David's vast stores.
Ellicott shows the verse an almost literal duplicate of 1 Kings 7:51, renders the 'hallowed vessels' of David, and marks the opening of the great dedication section.

themes: the ark as type of Christ

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the transfer of the ark from Zion, the city of David, to the temple, note the accidental loss of the month-name Ethanim, fix the feast in the seventh month, and render 'the chiefs of the clans.'

Keil harmonises the accounts: the 'Levites' who bore the ark were Levites who were also priests, while the ordinary Levites carried the dismantled tabernacle; 'the priests the Levites' is corrected to 'the priests and the Levites.'
Clarke fixes the occasion as the feast of Tabernacles in the seventh month, the autumn festival of ingathering.
Ellicott resolves the Levites/priests variant by the breadth of the tribal name Levi, which embraces the Aaronites; v.7 shows the priests were intended.

themes: the breadth of the tribal name

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott expound the graded procession — priests bearing the ark and vessels, Levites the tabernacle-relic — and correct 'the priests the Levites' to 'the priests and the Levites' from 1 Kings 8:4.
Wesley reads the ark as a type of Christ and the token of God's presence, and notes the old vessels reverently preserved.

themes: the ark and the tabernacle brought up
the ark as type of Christ

schools.summary: Ellicott marks that the verb 'assembled' here means to meet at an appointed place (as God meets Israel at the ark, Exodus 25:22), and reads a continuous 'were sacrificing' of the countless victims, king and congregation together before the ark.

Ellicott reads 'the cherubim were spreading forth wings,' notes that Kings' 'for' makes the clause explain v.7, and that 'covered' differs from the technical overshadowing term (Exodus 25:20; 1 Chron. 28:18) by a single transposed letter — the great cherubim arching over the ark that bore its own golden pair.
Clarke: the staves were drawn out as no longer needed; Wesley fixes 'unto this day' to the day the history was first written, before ark and staves were lost in Babylon.

Ellicott renders the long staves visible before the oracle, prefers the reading that the staves (not the ark) 'were there,' and sees the unaltered 'unto this day' as the mark of a source copied with literal fidelity.

debates.question: Does 'and there it is unto this day' speak of the ark, or of the staves?

sides.position: the ark remained there (the Vulgate and the received Hebrew)
the staves were there, agreeing with 1 Kings 8:8

schools.summary: Clarke reports the Chaldee paraphrase: nothing in the ark but the second tables Moses placed there after the first were broken over the calf at Horeb — a monument of mercy after apostasy.
Ellicott notes the Chronicler's 'put' for 'placed' (cf. Exodus 40:20) and reads the two tables as the covenant itself (cf. 2 Chron. 6:11; 1 Kings 8:21).

themes: nothing in the ark but the tables

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott expound the Chronicler's own parenthesis on the Levitical ministry: all the priestly courses, not merely the week's, sanctified themselves for so great a festival.
Wesley distinguishes ordinary service by course from extraordinary solemnities when all came together; Clarke sets the priests' exit after the ark was housed.
Henry reads the priests' withdrawal as the way of giving possession — room made for God — and marks that the cloud came at praise, not sacrifice.

Keil and Ellicott catalogue the singers of Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun in white linen with cymbals, lutes, and lyres, and the 120 trumpeting priests (five to each course), Ellicott noting the Chronicler's characteristic style.
Clarke doubts the combined instruments and 120 trumpets could yield any harmony or melody.

outliers.note: Judges that cymbals, psalteries, and harps joined to 120 trumpets could have produced no harmony and no melody, the noise being too great — a dissent on the music's effect the other witnesses do not share.

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott stress the emphatic unison — trumpeters and singers 'as one, with one sound' — singing 'for he is good, his mercy endureth for ever,' at which the cloud filled the house, the bright token of Jehovah's presence.

themes: the singers and trumpeters as one

schools.summary: Keil ties the verse to 1 Kings 8:11; Ellicott glosses 'stand to minister' as drawing near to serve, comparing 2 Chron. 7:2 where the same glory bars the priests, and notes 'house of God' for 'house of the LORD.'
Clarke breaks into wonder at the proof of the divine presence that stopped the priests' ministry.

Henry reads the glory-cloud as God taking possession and accepting the house (as the tabernacle in Exodus 40:34), veiled for human weakness (Job 26:9), and the priests' withdrawal as a foretoken that the Levitical priesthood must cease when Messiah comes (Malachi 3:1).
